title: Day 23 — Production Ready Frontend
summary: 15-minute review only: choose one previous project, refactor for production — folder structure, hooks, performance, accessibility, TypeScript, error handling — then defend every decision in a senior architecture discussion.

blocks.content: You have built weather dashboards, movie search, shopping carts, theme systems, Redux todos, RTK Query products, auth flows, admin dashboards, and Kanban boards. Today you stop building new features and start thinking like a senior engineer shipping to production. Pick ONE previous project. Refactor it. Then explain every decision as if a staff engineer is reviewing your PR and your architecture doc.

blocks.title: No new React concepts
blocks.content: Everything you apply today was taught in Weeks 1–3. This is refactor and explain only — not a day to learn new libraries, patterns, or frameworks. If you reach for something outside the curriculum, stop and use what you already know.
Skim this lesson once. Do not read new documentation. You already know the material — today is about applying it under production standards and articulating trade-offs.

items.text: Recall feature-based folder structure from Week 2 and Week 3 projects
List custom hooks you have written: useFetch, useDebounce, useTheme, useAuth
Refresh Context vs Redux vs RTK Query decision matrix from Week 3
Review React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, lazy, Suspense from Day 20
Remember: buttons are buttons, inputs have labels, errors are announced

rows: Custom hooks, API error states, responsive layout
Extract useWeather hook, typed API models, loading skeleton
Split SearchBar and ResultsList, memoize MovieCard
Typed cart slice, reusable CartItem, accessibility on quantity controls
Feature folder, typed selectors, error boundary on async thunk
Separate UI types from API types, lazy-load admin routes
AuthContext cleanup, loading gate, session error handling
Machine coding structure under refactor lens
Extract hooks, performance on long lists, a11y on drag/move

blocks.title: Pick the project you are weakest at explaining
blocks.content: Interviewers will drill your portfolio project. Refactoring your weakest build gives you the most interview ROI before tomorrow's mock.
Work through each axis. Not every item needs a change — but every item needs a conscious decision you can defend in an interview.

items.text: Folder structure — feature folders with colocated components, hooks, api, types
Reusable components — extract shared UI used 2+ times; no copy-pasted JSX
Custom hooks — extract data fetching, form logic, and side effects from components
Performance — profile first; apply memo, useMemo, useCallback, lazy only where measured
Accessibility — semantic HTML, labels, keyboard nav, focus management, aria-live errors
TypeScript — strict props interfaces, API models, no any, guard optional params
Code quality — consistent naming, single responsibility, no dead code
Error handling — try/catch on fetch, user-facing error UI, retry where appropriate
Loading states — skeletons or spinners on every async path; no layout shift surprises
Responsive design — usable on mobile; no horizontal scroll traps; touch targets sized

blocks.text: Performance Checklist — Apply Only Where Appropriate

blocks.title: Senior rule
blocks.content: Premature optimization fails interviews. Profile with React DevTools first. Document why you applied each optimization — or why you skipped it.

rows: Pure child re-renders often with same props; expensive render
Leaf components that always get new props; cheap renders
Memo prevents re-render when props are shallow-equal
Expensive filter/sort on large lists; stable object for context value
Simple arithmetic; values that change every render anyway
Caches computed value between renders when deps unchanged
Handler passed to memoized child; effect dependency needs stable ref
Handler not passed down; child not memoized
Returns same function reference when deps unchanged
Heavy routes or modals not needed on first paint
React.lazy + Suspense defers chunk download until needed
Route-level or feature-level bundles reduce initial JS
Tiny apps where one bundle is already small
Dynamic import() creates separate webpack/vite chunks

blocks.title: Document your decisions
blocks.content: For each optimization you apply, write one sentence: what you measured, what you changed, what improved. For each you skip, write why it was unnecessary. This becomes your architecture discussion script.
After refactoring, prepare to answer these questions out loud. This is exactly what Round 4 on Day 24 looks like — except today you practice on your chosen project first.

Explain feature colocation: components, hooks, api, and types that change together live together. Shared atoms in components/ui. Routes in routes/. Avoid flat components/ with 40 unrelated files. Trade-off: slightly more nesting vs faster onboarding and safer refactors.

Context fits low-frequency global reads — theme, locale, auth session snapshot. Wrap provider high, memoize value object, expose custom hook (useTheme). Trade-off: all consumers re-render on value change unless split contexts or memoized — not for high-frequency updates like cart item counts at scale.

Redux Toolkit when multiple slices interact, you need middleware, time-travel DevTools, and predictable action flows — todos, cart, UI flags. Trade-off: boilerplate vs debuggability. Do not put server cache in Redux if RTK Query handles it.

RTK Query owns server state — caching, invalidation, loading/error per endpoint, deduplication. Use when already on Redux or you want unified store. Trade-off: learning curve vs rewriting fetch + useEffect + manual cache everywhere.

URL is shareable state — deep links, back button, protected routes with guards. useParams for dynamic segments, Navigate for redirects, Outlet for nested layouts. Trade-off: client-side routing needs SPA fallback config in production hosting.

Smart/container components fetch and pass data; presentational components render props. Single responsibility — TaskCard displays, TaskList orchestrates. Trade-off: more files vs easier testing and reuse. Do not over-split into one-line wrappers.

Name at least three honest trade-offs: speed of shipping vs test coverage, Context simplicity vs Redux scalability, client-side filter vs server pagination, memo overhead vs render savings. Senior engineers say what they would do differently with more time.

Virtualization for long lists, E2E tests with Playwright, runtime API validation with Zod, error monitoring with Sentry, i18n, design tokens. Show you think beyond the demo without over-engineering today.
Run the same code review you practiced on Day 22 — but this time compare your refactored project against the original. Write a before/after summary a senior engineer would accept in a PR description.

rows: Extract at second use, not first
Profiled; memo on hot path only

blocks.title: Architecture doc required
blocks.content: Write a short ARCHITECTURE.md or PR description covering: folder structure rationale, state management choices, performance decisions, trade-offs, and three future improvements. You will reuse this verbatim on Day 24 Round 4.

Block 45–60 minutes. Answer all 8 questions out loud as if a senior frontend engineer is probing your production refactor. Focus on architecture, performance, scalability, and maintainability — not trivia.
Tell me why you made this decision, what you traded away, and what you would improve with another sprint.

items.text: One previous project refactored with production checklist complete
Performance decisions documented — applied and skipped
Architecture discussion written — structure, state, trade-offs, future
8 senior interview questions answered out loud
Quiz passed
Refactored code pushed to Git
